questions.question: You include a bar graph in a quiz question asking students to identify a trend. What must you provide so a screen reader user can answer the question?

options.text: Detailed alt text describing the graph title, axes, and key data points — or a long description providing equivalent information
No alt text is needed because it is part of a quiz, not a document
A link to the raw data in a spreadsheet

questions.explanation: Images in quiz questions need alt text that is complete enough for a screen reader user to answer the question correctly. For a graph, this means including the title, what the axes represent, and the data points (or trends) needed to answer. Generic alt text like 'bar graph' does not provide enough information. A spreadsheet link might be helpful but is not a substitute for accessible alt text in the question itself.

questions.question: Why should mathematical equations in quizzes be written using MathML or LaTeX notation rather than as images?

options.text: Images of equations take up too much space on the page
Screen readers cannot read an image of an equation, but they can read MathML or LaTeX notation aloud
MathML and LaTeX are easier to type than taking a screenshot
Images are not allowed in quizzes according to WCAG standards

questions.explanation: Screen readers can interpret MathML and LaTeX (or similar semantic markup) and read mathematical notation aloud in a way that makes sense. A screen reader cannot read an image of an equation — it just says 'image.' For a student using a screen reader to answer a math question, semantic notation is essential. While other reasons (file size, ease of typing) might favor semantic notation, the accessibility reason is the primary one.
